text:
$AEHR  Aehr Test Systems

TLDR 5 reasons why AEHR will outperform the market
-Its tools stress semiconductors exposing failures before they become far more costly downstream.
-Customers are increasingly interested in components that will not fail
-One quarter of bookings exceeded the entire previous year’s revenue.
-AEHR orders should lead the optical revenue curve by several quarters.
-A bet in AEHR is a bet that optical test demand follows an S-curve over the next 12 months.

Napkin math:

• FY2026 revenue: $50M
• 2028 revenue ceiling: $471M (9.4x)
• Market cap: $2.3B at $72 close, ~$2.9B at ~$92 AH
• 2028 target: ~$5.7B / $177 per share
• Upside: 2.4x at close, ~1.9x AH

1/ Aehr builds wafer-level and package-level burn-in systems.
Its tools stress semiconductors before they enter expensive optical modules, AI accelerators and power systems, exposing latent failures before they become far more costly downstream.

2/ AI infrastructure is approaching the limits of copper.
1.6T optics, CPO and increasingly optical scale-up networks require massive volumes of lasers, photonic chips and optical engines.

Those devices need production test and burn-in capacity before they can ship.

3/ Aehr’s FOX platform burns in entire wafers in parallel.

That allows customers to remove weak devices before packaging and creates recurring demand for proprietary WaferPak contactors as the installed base expands.

-=-=-=-=-=-
AEHR orders should lead the optical revenue curve by several quarters.
-=-=-=-=-=-

4/ The July print confirmed the inflection:

• Q4 revenue: $18.8M
• Record bookings: $60.7M
• Effective backlog: $100.6M
• FY2027 guidance: $130M–$150M
• Non-GAAP net margin guide: 18%–22%

One quarter of bookings exceeded the entire previous year’s revenue.

https://t.co/9hGWkjfYZW

5/ The optical evidence matters more than the headline guide.
• Lead silicon-photonics customer is ramping
• Second global networking customer forecasts more systems this year
• Lead AI customer is moving accelerator burn-in from system level to all wafer level
• Another top-tier AI processor supplier completed successful benchmarking and requested pilot validation

6/ My 2028 revenue ceiling:

• Silicon-photonics WLBI: ~$225M
• AI-processor WLBI and Sonoma: ~$196M
• SiC, GaN and other: ~$50M
• Total: ~$471M
The margin path rises from 18% in Q3 2026 toward 40% in 2028 as volume and proprietary consumables scale.

7/ The market historically treated Aehr as a volatile SiC equipment company.
The new order book is increasingly driven by AI processors and silicon photonics.
The remaining alpha comes from estimating how quickly $100.6M of backlog and the optical qualification pipeline convert into revenue.

8/ Supply-chain position:

InP lasers: $LITE $COHR $SIVE

SiPh PICs: $TSEM
→ Wafer-level burn-in: $AEHR
→ Optical engines/modules: $AAOI $COHR $LITE
→ AI switches and processors: $NVDA $AVGO $MRVL

Test capacity must be installed before this chain can reach volume.

9/ Risks:
• Customer concentration remains high
• My $170M FY2027 ceiling exceeds the $150M guide
• Order recognition can move between quarters
• Sonoma competes with Advantest and Teradyne
• Q4 still showed a GAAP operating loss
• At ~$92 AH, the stock has little tolerance for a slow conversion
Counter-evidence: two photonics customers, a second AI-processor evaluation and record backlog.

10/ Catalysts:

• Additional FOX-XP orders from either photonics customer
• Pilot-production order from the newly benchmarked AI supplier
• FY2027 revenue tracking above $150M
• Consumables returning above 30% of revenue
• Operating-margin progression through each quarter

At ~$92, this is no longer a cheap discovery trade.
It is a bet that optical test demand follows an S-curve over the next 12 months.

I think it does, expectation is about a 2x over the next 12 months (from 92 to about 200)

Shares are now up more than 30% after hours.

text:
$TSM volume เยอะมากกกกก ผลิตไม่ทันครับ ทำให้หลาย hyperscaler ต้องหา Plan B  NPO บนแพลตฟอร์มของ $TSEM

$TSEM มี moat SiPho platform ของตัวเองด้วย ไม่ใช่แค่ OEM อย่างเดียว และ capacity ล้นต้องการ fab เพิ่ม

ต้นน้ำ $IQE => $TSEM
“ IQE ’s InP epiwafers will be used in several of Tower’s advanced silicon photonics platforms for next-generation optical technologies.”

- จาก press release ร่วมครัช

ใช้ผลิต 200G lane pluggable transceivers และอื่นๆ
ใช้ใน Optical Circuit Switches สำหรับ datacenter
TSEM ให้ IP แก่ IQE ด้วย license porous silicon patents

ซิลิคอนเก่งเรื่อง นำแสง (waveguide), ปรับแสง (modulator) และ ตรวจจับแสง (photodetector) มาก แต่ สร้างแสง (laser) ไม่ค่อยเก่ง เพราะโครงสร้างทางฟิสิกส์
InP เก่งเรื่องสร้างแสงมาก จึงต้องนำ InP มา heterogeneous integration กับ silicon platform ของ Tower เพื่อให้ได้ระบบที่สมบูรณ์แบบครับ

ส่วน $MRVL => $TSEM 
ไม่เกี่ยวกับข่าว plan B แต่ TSEM ผลิตของ MRVL 
volume เยอะมากๆๆและจะเยอะมหาศาลขึ้นอีก

text:
$AEHR The AI Thesis Keeps Playing Out Annnd Now SiC Is Reviving Too!!

AEHR guided FY2027 revenue to $ 130-150M. That’s 160-200% YoY growth, backed by a record ~$100.6 M effective backlog, not optimism, but visibility. 

They also expect non-GAAP net income at 18-22% of revenue.

Q4 already showed the swing: $18.8M revenue (+33% YoY), turned profitable ($1.4M GAAP / $3.6M non-GAAP), record $60.7M in bookings, cash jumped to $116.5 M.

The AI engine is confirming and expanding:
• Lead production customer ramping wafer-level burn-in for AI accelerators (training + inference) a structural content increase per chip.

• New top-tier AI supplier benchmark win now moving to pilot on a second device.

• Record follow-on Sonoma orders from lead hyperscaler for next-gen higher-power packages.

• Silicon photonics emerging as a third leg (new global networking customer + systems for optical I/O & data-center interconnects).

• Memory roadmap targeting NAND and potential high-bandwidth memory burn-in.

At the same time, the SiC business the market wrote off is coming back:

• >$8M new SiC wafer-level burn-in orders in the past month, including a direct order from one of the world’s largest automakers + first SiC customer in Taiwan.

This is no longer AI replacing SiC. It’s AI compounding while SiC recovers as a second engine underneath. Diversified across AI accelerators, photonics, memory, data-center power, with the EV-driven SiC business finally turning up.

Strong setup for a small-cap semi name with this level of visibility. The real risk is concentration in the lead AI customer. 

Key questions on the call: how much of the backlog sits with that one customer, is the benchmark win a new logo, and how the backlog splits across AI / SiC / photonics.

text:
$AEHR The Guidance: Management officially projected FY2027 revenue to be between $130 million and $150 million.
 Why it matters: Wall Street analysts were only modeling ~$82 million. AEHR's actual forecast represents a staggering 160% to 200% year-over-year growth trajectory.
 Profitability Margin: They also guided that non-GAAP net income will landing at 18% to 22% of total revenue.
What This Tells Us
The massive record bookings ($60.7 million) and total effective backlog ($100.6 million) prove that their pivot into custom ASIC package-level burn-in for hyperscale AI customers and silicon photonics isn't just a pilot project—it's scaling into high-volume manufacturing exceptionally fast. The supply chain bottleneck for AI infrastructure testing is officially paying off for them.

text:
$AEHR is up 30% after hours. 

It's certainly been a bumpy ride for photonics, but I think this ER is extremely bullish for the sector.  Congrats to the 3000+ investors who own this stock with me on @joinautopilot via my Photonics Is Next portfolio.

When @KawzInvests and I published our photonics testing deep dive, $AEHR had just printed a $10.3M revenue quarter and the market called it a miss. We said to watch the bookings, not the revenue.

Q4 settled it:

- Revenue of $18.8M, up 33% YoY
- Record bookings of $60.7M in a single quarter
- Effective backlog doubled in 90 days
- Back to GAAP profitability

Then the bomb. FY27 revenue guidance of $130M to $150M. That's 160% to 200% growth against $50M this year.

The drivers are exactly what we wrote about:

- Their lead AI customer is shifting all burn-in from system level to wafer level for its accelerators

- A benchmark with a major supplier of AI accelerators and CPUs exceeded expectations. They're now moving toward pilot production and handed $AEHR a second device to evaluate

- The lead silicon photonics customer is ramping with follow-on orders, and their newest customer, a global networking leader, is forecasting additional systems this year.

Remember the structural setup. Every silicon photonics wafer needs burn-in before packaging. $AEHR is still the only company doing this at production scale.

Everyone staring at 60x trailing sales missed that the guide compresses it to ~21x forward.

Super excited for photonics earnings season!

text:
$AEHR Q4 Performance & FY27 Outlook

​Financials

• ​Q4 Revenue: $19M (In-line).
• ​Q4 EPS: $0.11 (Beat vs. $0.01 loss estimate).

• ​Backlog: Strong position at $100M with $61M in new bookings.

• ​FY27 Guidance: $140M revenue (+180% YoY), significantly exceeding the $85M consensus.

​Key Drivers

• ​Silicon Photonics: Expanded to two customers, including a global networking leader, as AI data centers adopt optical I/O.

• ​Silicon Carbide: Added $8M in recent WaferPak orders, headlined by a top-three global automaker.

• ​Hyperscale (Sonoma): Record follow-on orders with a shift toward higher-power density devices.

text:
$AEHR delivers: Record $60.7M Q4 bookings, $100M + effective backlog, and FY2027 outlook of $130 - 150M — 2.6–3.0x fiscal 2026 revenue. Strong execution in AI processors and silicon photonics positions the company at the intersection of wafer-level test innovation and hyperscale infrastructure demand.

A name included in the Photonics basket , +32% aftermarket. Structural tailwinds building. 
$AEHR

Aehr Test Systems ($AEHR) — Ρεκόρ παραγγελιών, ισχυρό backlog και αισιόδοξο FY27 outlook

📊 Αποτελέσματα

Έσοδα: $18,8 εκατ. (+33,3% σε ετήσια βάση)
GAAP EPS: $0,04, έναντι -$0,10 πέρυσι
Non-GAAP EPS: $0,11, έναντι -$0,01 πέρυσι
Ρεκόρ παραγγελιών (Bookings): $60,7 εκατ.
Effective Backlog: $100,6 εκατ.

🎯 Guidance (FY27)

FY27 Revenue: $130M–$150M
Expected Revenue Growth: +160% έως +200% σε ετήσια βάση
FY27 Non-GAAP Net Income: 18%–22% του revenue

📌 Βασικά σημεία

Η ζήτηση για AI processors επιταχύνεται τόσο στο wafer-level burn-in όσο και στο package-level burn-in.
Μεγάλος προμηθευτής AI processors ολοκλήρωσε benchmark testing και εξετάζει την έναρξη πιλοτικής παραγωγής.
Πελάτες στον χώρο του silicon photonics αυξάνουν την παραγωγική τους δυναμικότητα για εφαρμογές AI optical interconnects.
Η Aehr έλαβε περίπου $8 εκατ. νέες παραγγελίες για silicon carbide μέσα στον τελευταίο μήνα.
Τα διαθέσιμα μετρητά και τα restricted cash αυξήθηκαν στα $116,5 εκατ.

💬 Σχόλιο διοίκησης

«Με πολλούς πελάτες να εισέρχονται ή να επεκτείνουν την παραγωγή τους, με ρεκόρ backlog και με πρόσθετες ευκαιρίες τόσο στο wafer-level όσο και στο package-level burn-in, πιστεύουμε ότι η Aehr είναι τοποθετημένη για αρκετά χρόνια ισχυρής ανάπτυξης εσόδων.»

Τα διαθέσιμα μετρητά και τα restricted cash αυξήθηκαν στα $116,5 εκατ. 💬 Σχόλιο διοίκησης «Με πολλούς πελάτες να εισέρχονται ή να επεκτείνουν την παραγωγή τους, με ρεκόρ backlog και με πρόσθετες ευκαιρίες τόσο στο wafer-level όσο και στο package-level burn-in, πιστεύουμε ότι η Aehr είναι τοποθετημένη για αρκετά χρόνια ισχυρής ανάπτυξης εσόδων.»"}}}, "rest_id": "2077141892991434998", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "96", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$AEHR just guided FY2027 revenue to $130M - $150M, up 160% to 200% from the $50M it printed in FY2026. 

Q4 came in at $18.8M with $0.11 adjusted EPS against a consensus loss, record bookings of $60.7M and an effective backlog of $100.6M 

The driver is silicon photonics burn in finally hitting production scale. Aehr’s lead SiPho customer keeps placing follow on orders for fully automated wafer level burn in systems, and a second major networking customer is ramping capacity. 

Every 800G/1.6T transceiver and optical I/O chip going into AI data centers needs reliability screening, and Aehr owns that step of the process.

This is the test layer of the optical interconnect buildout monetizing. Transceiver volume growth flows straight through to burn in capacity demand, and with AI processors and power semis stacked on top, the backlog gives unusual visibility for a company this size.

Stock seems to like it too

text:
During this "everything" crash, I'm focused on strong AI themes: memory, neoclouds, photonics, and robotics.

Neocloud stocks like $WYFI, $WULF, $NBIS, $IREN, $CRWV, $CIFR, etc. may continue to experience turbulence if $WGMI can't close above weekly gold.

Positioned for any scenario.

If it holds, then we're good to go.

If it doesn't hold and it's time to derisk, then we can start getting excited about accumulating our favorite names at increasingly better prices.

I completely agree... the volatility in high beta names can be scary, especially when a big stock market index like the KOSPI is down just as much!

But I'll never stay quiet on a red day.

Neocloud fundamentals remain rock solid... with constant tailwinds like the recent $CLSK deal and many others.

Things may look bleak now but the future has never been brighter IMO.

Just gotta focus on survival. 🙏

Will highlight entries as they come.

text:
Did You Know?

The biggest challenge facing AI isn't building faster processors. It's moving information between them.

As AI clusters scale, traditional copper interconnects are increasingly constrained by bandwidth, power consumption, and thermal performance. Conventional optical links extend reach but add complexity, higher power demands, and greater cost.

That's why Kopin and FabricAI are developing Neural I/o™, a MicroLED-based optical interconnect technology designed to adress the bandwidth, latency, and power challenges shaping the next generation of AI infrastructure.

The biggest challenge facing AI isn't building faster processors. It's moving information between them. As AI clusters scale, traditional copper interconnects are increasingly constrained by bandwidth, power consumption, and thermal performance. Conventional optical links extend reach but add complexity, higher power demands, and greater cost. That's why Kopin and FabricAI are developing Neural I/o™, a MicroLED-based optical interconnect technology designed to adress the bandwidth, latency, and power challenges shaping the next generation of AI infrastructure. text:
In my eyes, the selloff in high-beta stocks is mostly sentiment driven, and not driven by fundamental changes.

We have seen some FUD lines in the past few weeks:
> $META announcing they will rent out excess capacity compute. Panic across the board because people think they will reduce CAPEX. But it is quite the opposite actually according to the latest news.

> CXMT IPO and the fear that CXMT and YMTC will flood the Western market with cheap memory. Complete utter nonsense, they can barely supply the domestic market and Western hyperscalers and CSPs are not going to use Chinese memory.

> SemiAnalysis CPO report was very sus. The fact that they published a bearish report and formed a photonics ETF after the sector dropped 40-50% makes me wary.

In my eyes, the selloff in the AI infrastructure sector with $AAOI, $SNDK, $MU, $SKHY, $KXIAY, you name it, is mostly driven by sentiment, not by fundamentals.

Sentiment may dictate the price action in the short-term, in the longer term fundamentals will prevail.

text:
Join us at ADLM 2026 as the global laboratory medicine and point-of-care community comes together.  

Hamamatsu will exhibit an assortment of high performance, miniature photonic solutions designed to bring laboratory testing to the point-of-care market. Hamamatsu will show state-of-the-art sensor modules based on photon counting and mini-spectrometers that provide ultimate sensitivity along with industry leading reliability, a combination critical in healthcare diagnostic systems.  

Connect with the Hamamatsu team at ADLM and explore what’s next in diagnostic innovation. Learn more about the event: https://t.co/5Odd8Dag6q  

Book an appointment with our experts: https://t.co/Fq6AIdSWth

#ADLM2026 #Photonics #LifeSciences #PointofCare #OpticsEngineering

text:
very out of touch

the top 2 labs can’t imagine that spending vast sums to inefficiently race for the frontier only guarantees market capture at best, and is only the best strategy when guaranteed infinite funding

IMO, they’re just shocked that open source can eat their lunch, while they’re holding shackles from their wasteful race, not even considering prioritizing a holistic efficiency research program until 2025

also, with next-gen parallel processing tech from Light-matter, Extropic, and even Nvidia coming down the pipe (photonics, analog)…

it’s time for the shaky leaders to digest the pill, not just hold it in their mouth

In my fairly limited use, it also", "id_str": "2078133895766114412", "is_quote_status": false, "lang": "en", "quote_count": 1739, "reply_count": 2078, "retweet_count": 909, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "30557408"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [], "urls": [], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzgxMzM4OTU0MDk1ODIwODA=", "media": {"inline_media": []}, "richtext": {"richtext_tags": []}, "text": "Some observations on Kimi: 1. It's a very good model! I don't think its performance can be explained away by distillation or anything like that. In agentic coding sessions, it seems pretty much on par with the best public models of Q1 2026. In my fairly limited use, it also seemed very token hungry. It's not obvious to me that this model is actually that cheap to run. 2. I am personally surprised the Chinese state continues to allow the open sourcing of models this good, given potential risks. To be clear, I *myself* might be fine with models presenting this level of marginal risk being open weight, but I am surprised that China is fine with it. I suspect the reason they are is 75% explained by strategic blindness/lack of AGI-pilledness (the CCP is very Yann Lecun-y in its views of AI). The other 25% or so is their lack of compute for customer inference (making China's open-weight strategy an unintended byproduct of US export controls) and the normal Chinese strategy of aggressive exports. For the companies, as opposed to the government, the decision to open source is partially ideological and partially because they are behind, and they know that very few people would pay for sub-frontier models from China. 3. Open-weight models are inherently decelerationist, and I'm continually surprised to see the so-called "accelerationists" so excited about open-weight models. I suspect the reason they are is that they know open-weight models are effectively ungovernable, and they simply like the overall cloak of ungovernability open-weight models create over the whole of AI. It's not a bad strategy; it reminds me of James Scott's recounting of the hill people in "the art of not being governed." Still, in the end, open-weight models deter further AI capex. 4. One probable outcome of an open-weight-model-dominant world is full AI communism, which is precisely what China proposes: rather than a market product, AI is a "public good" which will ultimately be provided by the state as a kind of "digital public infrastructure." This future strikes me as a dystopian hellscape, but I've never met an open-weight models advocate who doesn't ultimately concede this is where things end. You'd be surprised how many 'accelerationists' lobbied me, while I was in government, to support an eleven or twelve-figure federally funded data center so that startups could train models at a subsidy and then give them away for free. There was no other way for AI to progress, they said. Perhaps this is the logical end state of things. Nonetheless, I find myself surprised to see supposed accelerationists excited about such an outcome. I think many of them just don't know what they're doing. Many accelerationists do not view the creation and serving of frontier models as a legitimate business. 5. I would guess that the Trump Administration will at some point realize that their best strategy here would be to create large amounts of regulatory risk around the use of open-weight Chinese models. You don't need to "ban open source" (one of the dumber motifs of AI policy discussion). You just need to direct every agency to issue soft law that creates FUD. "A Federal Reserve Advisory Bulletin found that there may be backdoors in Chinese AI models." It needn't be that well justified. You just create enough regulatory risk that every regulated enterprise backs off. You probably don't want to create so much regulatory risk that you scare off the hyperscalers from serving Chinese models; this will just drive startups to sketchier providers. There's a happy middle ground here. I'd assume they will do some version of this. 6. It's probably true that open-weight models of this capability make the world a bit more dangerous, but not so much more that you'll really notice. At some point the models will be capable enough that you will notice. "A nonliving, invisible, dangerous, and infinitely self-replicating agent escaped from a Chinese lab," you say? Color me shocked."}}}, "rest_id": "2078133895766114412", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "10881961", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2079266068871450904", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "40", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text: A fibre thinner than a human hair can tell you exactly how a structure is bending in real time. Fibre Bragg Gratings shift their reflected wavelength in direct proportion to strain — precise enough to detect sub-millimetre deformation. This is how future infrastructure monitors itself. #FBG #Photonics #StructuralHealthMonitoring #Sensing

text:
$LWLG Strengthens Leadership With Veteran CFO as It Prepares for the Next Phase of Growth

$LWLG announced the appointment of Fred Graffam as its new Chief Financial Officer, bringing more than 20 years of financial leadership and extensive public company CFO experience to the photonics company.

While a CFO appointment doesn’t change the company’s technology overnight, it often signals that management is preparing for the next stage of corporate growth, partnerships, and investor engagement.

Graffam has led finance organizations at several public companies, including Fidium (formerly Consolidated Communications), Ascent Capital Group, Brinks Home Security, and DigitalGlobe, where he helped oversee strategic transactions and corporate sales. His background also includes leadership roles at Level 3 Communications, Comcast, and Deloitte.

CEO Yves LeMaitre said Graffam’s experience in financial operations, capital allocation, and communicating with Wall Street will help support the company’s long-term strategy and create shareholder value.

Graffam also expressed confidence in Lightwave Logic’s future, describing the company as being at a “significant inflection point.” He believes its proprietary electro-optic (EO) polymer technology could play an important role in next-generation optical networking as AI drives demand for faster, lower-power data transmission.

Why It Matters

As AI models become larger and data centers continue to scale, moving information efficiently is becoming just as important as increasing computing power.

Lightwave Logic is developing electro-optic polymers that convert electrical signals into optical signals with higher speed and lower power consumption than conventional technologies. If successfully commercialized, the technology could improve optical interconnects used in AI servers, networking equipment, and telecommunications infrastructure.

However, investors should remember that $LWLG remains an early-stage technology company. The long-term opportunity depends on converting years of research into commercial products, customer agreements, and sustainable revenue.

My View

A new CFO doesn’t immediately change the investment thesis, but hiring an executive with significant public company and M&A experience is generally a positive step. It suggests management is strengthening its leadership team as the company moves closer to potential commercialization and deeper engagement with customers and investors.

Ultimately, the biggest catalysts for $LWLG will still be commercial partnerships, customer adoption, licensing agreements, and revenue growth. If the company can successfully translate its photonics technology into real-world deployments, it could become a niche beneficiary of the expanding AI networking and optical communications market.

$LWLG $POET $LITE $COHR $NVDA $AVGO $AMD

Leadership With Veteran CFO as It Prepares for the Next Phase of Growth $LWLG announced the appointment of Fred Graffam as its new Chief Financial Officer, bringing more than 20 years of financial leadership and extensive public company CFO experience to the photonics company. While a CFO appointment doesn’t change the company’s technology overnight, it often signals that management is preparing for the next stage of corporate growth, partnerships, and investor engagement. Graffam has led finance organizations at several public companies, including Fidium (formerly Consolidated Communications), Ascent Capital Group, Brinks Home Security, and DigitalGlobe, where he helped oversee strategic transactions and corporate sales. His background also includes leadership roles at Level 3 Communications, Comcast, and Deloitte. CEO Yves LeMaitre said Graffam’s experience in financial operations, capital allocation, and communicating with Wall Street will help support the company’s long-term strategy and create shareholder value. Graffam also expressed confidence in Lightwave Logic’s future, describing the company as being at a “significant inflection point.” He believes its proprietary electro-optic (EO) polymer technology could play an important role in next-generation optical networking as AI drives demand for faster, lower-power data transmission. Why It Matters As AI models become larger and data centers continue to scale, moving information efficiently is becoming just as important as increasing computing power. Lightwave Logic is developing electro-optic polymers that convert electrical signals into optical signals with higher speed and lower power consumption than conventional technologies. If successfully commercialized, the technology could improve optical interconnects used in AI servers, networking equipment, and telecommunications infrastructure. However, investors should remember that $LWLG remains an early-stage technology company. The long-term opportunity depends on converting years of research into commercial products, customer agreements, and sustainable revenue. My View A new CFO doesn’t immediately change the investment thesis, but hiring an executive with significant public company and M&A experience is generally a positive step. It suggests management is strengthening its leadership team as the company moves closer to potential commercialization and deeper engagement with customers and investors. Ultimately, the biggest catalysts for $LWLG will still be commercial partnerships, customer adoption, licensing agreements, and revenue growth. If the company can successfully translate its photonics technology into real-world deployments, it could become a niche beneficiary of the expanding AI networking and optical communications market. $LWLG $POET $LITE $COHR $NVDA $AVGO $AMD"}}}, "rest_id": "2079255598064185417", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "445", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
$LPTH Continues to Gain Wall Street Support as Demand for Advanced Optics Accelerates

As AI infrastructure expands beyond chips and data centers, another segment of the supply chain is attracting growing attention: precision optics and photonics.

That’s one reason Craig-Hallum has reiterated its Buy rating on $LPTH, while Wall Street’s consensus price target stands at $16.17, implying meaningful upside from the stock’s recent close of $10.53.

AI Hardware Requires More Than Just GPUs

Building AI infrastructure isn’t only about faster processors.

Modern AI systems also depend on advanced optical technologies used in:

High-speed data communications
Infrared imaging
Autonomous systems
Aerospace and defense
Industrial sensing
Semiconductor manufacturing

As computing power increases, demand for precision optical components continues to grow across multiple industries.

This creates a favorable backdrop for companies like Lightpath Technologies.

Revenue Is Growing Rapidly

Lightpath’s latest quarterly results showed significant top-line momentum.

Compared with the same quarter last year:

Revenue increased from $9.17 million to $19.15 million, more than doubling year over year.
However, the company still reported a GAAP net loss of $4.11 million, compared with a $3.58 million loss a year earlier.

The results suggest that while demand is improving, the business is still investing heavily to support future growth.

Profitability Remains the Next Milestone

Like many smaller technology companies, Lightpath is still in an investment phase.

Revenue growth is encouraging, but investors will ultimately want to see:

Improving gross margins
Better operating leverage
Reduced cash burn
A clearer path toward consistent profitability

Strong revenue alone is unlikely to sustain a higher valuation if losses continue to widen.

Insider Selling Deserves Context

One factor investors may notice is recent insider activity.

A director-affiliated investment fund recently sold approximately 3.57 million shares, worth about $50 million.

Large insider sales often attract attention, but they don’t necessarily indicate deteriorating business fundamentals.

Institutional funds may sell for reasons unrelated to the company’s outlook, including portfolio rebalancing, fund liquidity, or investment lifecycle considerations.

Investors should watch whether management’s operational execution continues to improve rather than relying solely on insider transactions.

My View

Lightpath sits in an interesting niche of the AI ecosystem.

While companies like $NVDA and $AMD provide the computing power, optical technologies are becoming increasingly important for moving data efficiently, supporting advanced sensors, and enabling next-generation AI hardware.

The company’s strong revenue growth suggests demand is improving, but the next stage of the investment story will depend on translating that growth into sustainable profits.

If management can continue expanding sales while improving margins, $LPTH could become one of the lesser-known beneficiaries of the broader AI infrastructure buildout.

text:
The European Commission has awarded funding to ELIgnite, a new Horizon Europe project coordinated by the ELI - Extreme Light Infrastructure.

Read more here: https://t.co/0SpUqSpvE9

#Photonics #Lasers

其实我们去看 $LITE 的市值变化会发现很有趣的事情：二十五年前它在泡沫里亏掉560亿，在25年初市值仅60亿美金，今天它却值六七百亿，而去研究他的发家史，我会不自觉的套用到 $sive身上。

2001年，一家叫JDS Uniphase的光通信巨头，一年亏了大约560亿美金。真金白银的年度亏损，上一轮互联网泡沫最大的墓碑之一。

今天我聊它是想去看懂这一轮AI里最容易被忽略的那条暗线；二是说句实话，我在拿它当一面镜子，去对照我最喜欢的一家公司 $SIVE。

这条暗线是：所有人都在抢光模块，可那颗藏在光模块里、最难造、连英伟达都得排队抢的激光芯片，是它供的。做光模块的公司被当"卖铲人"炒。可光模块拆开，里面那颗叫EML的铟磷激光心脏，五到六成出自Lumentum。它是卖铲人的卖铲人，那么硬到什么程度？2026年3月，英伟达直接掏20亿美金入股它、外加一份数十亿的采购大单，就为把它的激光产能提前锁死。全世界最懂算力供需的那家，用钱投了一票"这颗芯片会长期缺"。

但我更想让你注意的是它年轻时候的样子，因为它不是暴发户，是熬了几十年的老鬼。

1979年就有的血统，1999年合并成JDSU是那轮光纤狂欢里最亮的星，然后泡沫破了，亏掉560亿。2015年，它被当成一块"不性感的器件厂"分拆出来单过——给电信商供激光器、给苹果供Face ID的激光点阵，收入不小，故事平平，没人给估值。

接下来那十年才是关键。它趴在冷板凳上，靠一串"补器官"式的收购一块一块把造高端激光器需要的硬骨头焊进自己家：2018年吞Oclaro拿下相干光、2022年买NeoPhotonics拿下超窄线宽激光、2023年收Cloud Light一脚踏进数据中心光模块。到2023年底，家底攒齐了，可市值还只有35亿美金，没几个人拿正眼看它。

然后2025年2月，一个在光通信圈摸爬滚打小半辈子的老兵接过方向盘。几个月后，AI这阵风吹来了。结果就是那根鞭子：从2023年底约35亿，到2026年中六七百亿，不到三年，翻了18到20倍。

那就很好奇了它凭什么能被一把抡起来？

它是少数几个"手里真有货"的人。这十年的攒货，我在文里从头捋到尾。

现在说第二件也是我今天想聊的。

Lumentum年轻时候那个样子，和我买入的 $SIVE 现在像得让我后背发凉。

它俩做的是同一层的活：光互联最上游那颗铟磷激光光源。摩根士丹利那份"激光供应"名单本来就把SIVE和 $lite、 $COHR 归在一起。区别只在于时间——Lumentum是"跑通之后"的样子，SIVE是它十年前那个"手里正在攒货"的样子。
而且别以为SIVE是个刚冒头的初创。它真正对标Lumentum的那条激光光源命脉——Sivers Photonics，前身叫CST Global——是2001年从苏格兰格拉斯哥大学分拆出来的，做铟磷/DFB激光已经二十五年了，2016年就能月产上百万颗DFB激光器。Sivers这家公司2018年上了纳斯达克北欧的First North、2021年登上斯德哥尔摩主板。

不知道你看出来了吗：Lumentum是四十年的老手，SIVE的激光那条线也是二十五年的老手。 两个都不是暴发户，都是在这颗又脆又难的铟磷激光上磨了大半辈子的匠人：一个已经等到了风，一个还在等。这才是我把它俩摆在一起的真正原因。

你把两条路叠一起看，相似的地方太多了：

一样是那颗最难的InP激光光源、一样是几十年的老手；一样是小市值、没人看好、故事还没被主流讲透；一样在闷头攒家底——Lumentum当年靠收购，SIVE现在靠融资扩产能、靠一个个设计导入把自己焊进下一代CPO；也一样，都在等那个能把它一把抡起来的临界点。

Lumentum的临界点是英伟达那20亿 + AI那阵风。而SIVE 还在等它自己的那阵风，而目前我认为风来了（那一笔融资）

所以Lumentum这条18倍的路对我判断SIVE有两层意义：它先替我证实了这条赛道到底值不值钱（值），又告诉我SIVE想复制它，还差哪几步。我把SIVE现在走到了哪一格、还欠着哪几张"兑现凭证"，画成了一张LITE照SIVE的对照图——具体哪几步，我留在文里，这里先不摊。

我前几天算SIVE从10亿到100亿是10倍。听着激进？可Lumentum刚刚当着所有人的面，走完了18到20倍。有些倍数，不是幻想，是已经发生过的先例。

至于Lumentum现在——太贵了，市销率约25倍、同行最贵，把未来两三年的好消息都提前兑现了，所以它对我的价值从来不是"买不买它"，而是"照SIVE"。
完整的来时路、它到底跟谁在合作、以及那张LITE照SIVE的对照图，都在这一篇里。

"quote_count": 4, "reply_count": 8, "retweet_count": 15, "retweeted": false, "user_id_str": "923960315343245314"}, "note_tweet": {"is_expandable": true, "note_tweet_results": {"result": {"entity_set": {"hashtags": [], "symbols": [{"indices": [7, 12], "text": "LITE"}, {"indices": [960, 965], "text": "SIVE"}, {"indices": [1037, 1042], "text": "COHR"}, {"indices": [1964, 1969], "text": "LITE"}], "urls": [{"display_url": "substack.com/@markcherrypay…", "expanded_url": "https://substack.com/@markcherrypayment/note/p-207790901?r=8gv6xw&utm_medium=ios&utm_source=notes-share-action", "indices": [2011, 2034], "url": "https://t.co/3hqKvt3On9"}], "user_mentions": []}, "id": "Tm90ZVR3ZWV0OjIwNzkyNDYzOTUyMzY5MDkwNTY=", "text": "其实我们去看 $LITE 的市值变化会发现很有趣的事情：二十五年前它在泡沫里亏掉560亿，在25年初市值仅60亿美金，今天它却值六七百亿，而去研究他的发家史，我会不自觉的套用到 $sive身上。 2001年，一家叫JDS Uniphase的光通信巨头，一年亏了大约560亿美金。真金白银的年度亏损，上一轮互联网泡沫最大的墓碑之一。 今天我聊它是想去看懂这一轮AI里最容易被忽略的那条暗线；二是说句实话，我在拿它当一面镜子，去对照我最喜欢的一家公司 $SIVE。 这条暗线是：所有人都在抢光模块，可那颗藏在光模块里、最难造、连英伟达都得排队抢的激光芯片，是它供的。做光模块的公司被当"卖铲人"炒。可光模块拆开，里面那颗叫EML的铟磷激光心脏，五到六成出自Lumentum。它是卖铲人的卖铲人，那么硬到什么程度？2026年3月，英伟达直接掏20亿美金入股它、外加一份数十亿的采购大单，就为把它的激光产能提前锁死。全世界最懂算力供需的那家，用钱投了一票"这颗芯片会长期缺"。 但我更想让你注意的是它年轻时候的样子，因为它不是暴发户，是熬了几十年的老鬼。 1979年就有的血统，1999年合并成JDSU是那轮光纤狂欢里最亮的星，然后泡沫破了，亏掉560亿。2015年，它被当成一块"不性感的器件厂"分拆出来单过——给电信商供激光器、给苹果供Face ID的激光点阵，收入不小，故事平平，没人给估值。 接下来那十年才是关键。它趴在冷板凳上，靠一串"补器官"式的收购一块一块把造高端激光器需要的硬骨头焊进自己家：2018年吞Oclaro拿下相干光、2022年买NeoPhotonics拿下超窄线宽激光、2023年收Cloud Light一脚踏进数据中心光模块。到2023年底，家底攒齐了，可市值还只有35亿美金，没几个人拿正眼看它。 然后2025年2月，一个在光通信圈摸爬滚打小半辈子的老兵接过方向盘。几个月后，AI这阵风吹来了。结果就是那根鞭子：从2023年底约35亿，到2026年中六七百亿，不到三年，翻了18到20倍。 那就很好奇了它凭什么能被一把抡起来？ 它是少数几个"手里真有货"的人。这十年的攒货，我在文里从头捋到尾。 现在说第二件也是我今天想聊的。 Lumentum年轻时候那个样子，和我买入的 $SIVE 现在像得让我后背发凉。 它俩做的是同一层的活：光互联最上游那颗铟磷激光光源。摩根士丹利那份"激光供应"名单本来就把SIVE和 $lite、 $COHR 归在一起。区别只在于时间——Lumentum是"跑通之后"的样子，SIVE是它十年前那个"手里正在攒货"的样子。 而且别以为SIVE是个刚冒头的初创。它真正对标Lumentum的那条激光光源命脉——Sivers Photonics，前身叫CST Global——是2001年从苏格兰格拉斯哥大学分拆出来的，做铟磷/DFB激光已经二十五年了，2016年就能月产上百万颗DFB激光器。Sivers这家公司2018年上了纳斯达克北欧的First North、2021年登上斯德哥尔摩主板。 不知道你看出来了吗：Lumentum是四十年的老手，SIVE的激光那条线也是二十五年的老手。 两个都不是暴发户，都是在这颗又脆又难的铟磷激光上磨了大半辈子的匠人：一个已经等到了风，一个还在等。这才是我把它俩摆在一起的真正原因。 你把两条路叠一起看，相似的地方太多了： 一样是那颗最难的InP激光光源、一样是几十年的老手；一样是小市值、没人看好、故事还没被主流讲透；一样在闷头攒家底——Lumentum当年靠收购，SIVE现在靠融资扩产能、靠一个个设计导入把自己焊进下一代CPO；也一样，都在等那个能把它一把抡起来的临界点。 Lumentum的临界点是英伟达那20亿 + AI那阵风。而SIVE 还在等它自己的那阵风，而目前我认为风来了（那一笔融资） 所以Lumentum这条18倍的路对我判断SIVE有两层意义：它先替我证实了这条赛道到底值不值钱（值），又告诉我SIVE想复制它，还差哪几步。我把SIVE现在走到了哪一格、还欠着哪几张"兑现凭证"，画成了一张LITE照SIVE的对照图——具体哪几步，我留在文里，这里先不摊。 我前几天算SIVE从10亿到100亿是10倍。听着激进？可Lumentum刚刚当着所有人的面，走完了18到20倍。有些倍数，不是幻想，是已经发生过的先例。 至于Lumentum现在——太贵了，市销率约25倍、同行最贵，把未来两三年的好消息都提前兑现了，所以它对我的价值从来不是"买不买它"，而是"照SIVE"。 完整的来时路、它到底跟谁在合作、以及那张LITE照SIVE的对照图，都在这一篇里。 text:
I can understand all the possible AI chokepoints and bottlenecks...memory, photonics, robotics, semiconductors...but without energy, we aren't going very far. 
That is the first knot to untie.

That is why the market is pricing in increasingly higher value for companies in this sector.

text:
Побачити планету біля чужої зорі — задача на межі неможливого. Якщо планета в зоні придатності, вона тулиться близько до світила, а те світить приблизно в 10 мільярдів разів яскравіше. Стівен Айкенберрі, професор і керівник проєкту в оптичному коледжі Університету Центральної Флориди (CREOL), описує це так: спробуйте розгледіти крихітний блимаючий вогник, коли вам в обличчя б'є прожектор.

Астрономи вже гасять це сяйво коронографом — приладом, що перекриває диск зорі й пропускає до детектора слабкий сигнал планети. Та мікроскопічні дефекти оптики пропускають частину зоряного світла крізь усю систему. "Скажете — це ж одна мільйонна частка", — каже Айкенберрі. "І що? Зоря все одно в 10 тисяч разів яскравіша за вашу екзопланету. Вам кінець."

Проєкт PEEPSS (Photonics-Enabled Exoplanet Spectroscopic System) ловить ці спотворення там, де їх раніше не міряли — у фокальній площині, тій самій точці, де формується наукове зображення. Більшість систем стежить за хвильовим фронтом раніше в оптичному тракті й не бачить помилок, що виникають уже після коронографа. Їх називають аберраціями неспільного шляху: світло проходить ділянку, яку не контролює жоден датчик.

Серце проєкту — фотонний ліхтар. Пристрій розкладає вхідне світло на окремі оптичні канали й відновлює не лише яскравість, а й фазову інформацію хвиль — дані, які звичайні детектори просто стирають. Завдяки цьому відкривається "квантово-натхненне зображення", де поведінка світла підвищує роздільність і відфільтровує рештки зоряного сяйва. Передову роботу над такими ліхтарями у світі ведуть лише два центри — UCF та Університет Сіднея.

Підтверджених екзопланет уже понад 6 000, та пряме зображення досі бере хіба що газові гіганти розміром з Юпітер на далеких орбітах — землеподібні світи надто тьмяні. Технологію готують для майбутньої обсерваторії HWO (Habitable Worlds Observatory), флагманського телескопа NASA з дзеркалом 6–8 метрів для пошуку двійників Землі. Трирічний проєкт будує й тестує прототипи, а деякі версії вже випробували на телескопах на Гаваях. Айкенберрі формулює ставку прямо: "Ми за одну місію від відповіді. Підведемо очі — і знатимемо."

text:
A lab at Sandia National Laboratories just found a new strategy for controlling light. No human designed the experiment that found it.

If you run any kind of research or technology organization, that sentence should be sitting uncomfortably in the back of your planning assumptions right now.

Sandia scientists built what the field calls a self-driving lab — a closed-loop system where AI designs experiments, robotics runs them, and AI reads the results and designs the next one. The system isn't executing a pre-written protocol. It's building the protocol as it goes. And in the domain of optics and photonics, it returned with a novel light-control strategy the human researchers hadn't specified in advance.

Here's the part that matters for anyone with a five-year research roadmap: the self-driving lab doesn't just run known experiments faster. It explores the space between the experiments a human team would have prioritized. It goes places humans don't have bandwidth to go. That's a different category of capability than "AI-assisted workflow."

Photonics isn't niche — it's the foundation of fiber communications, lidar for autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, and solar energy systems. A new light-control strategy found autonomously has downstream implications across a dozen industries that are still treating AI adoption as a measured, multi-year transition.

The full piece on Substack is written directly to R&D directors, lab chiefs, and technology organization leaders — as a letter, not a lecture. It asks one genuinely hard question about your planning horizon and works through what the honest answer requires. Including the strongest case for why autonomous labs are still limited, and why that case actually sharpens the urgency rather than softening it.

After the article, Claude reviewed it independently and added its own labeled response. "The harder and more interesting question is whether the self-driving lab architecture can be extended to messy domains — not whether it can be deployed to easy ones."

No human designed the experiment that found it. If you run any kind of research or technology organization, that sentence should be sitting uncomfortably in the back of your planning assumptions right now. Sandia scientists built what the field calls a self-driving lab — a closed-loop system where AI designs experiments, robotics runs them, and AI reads the results and designs the next one. The system isn't executing a pre-written protocol. It's building the protocol as it goes. And in the domain of optics and photonics, it returned with a novel light-control strategy the human researchers hadn't specified in advance. Here's the part that matters for anyone with a five-year research roadmap: the self-driving lab doesn't just run known experiments faster. It explores the space between the experiments a human team would have prioritized. It goes places humans don't have bandwidth to go. That's a different category of capability than "AI-assisted workflow." Photonics isn't niche — it's the foundation of fiber communications, lidar for autonomous vehicles, medical imaging, and solar energy systems. A new light-control strategy found autonomously has downstream implications across a dozen industries that are still treating AI adoption as a measured, multi-year transition. The full piece on Substack is written directly to R&D directors, lab chiefs, and technology organization leaders — as a letter, not a lecture. It asks one genuinely hard question about your planning horizon and works through what the honest answer requires. Including the strongest case for why autonomous labs are still limited, and why that case actually sharpens the urgency rather than softening it.
